Name: dynamic_kvcache_layout
Title: Dynamic KV-Cache Layout Optimization for Hardware-Efficient Inference
Short Hypothesis: A dynamically adjustable KV-cache layout, guided by real-time profiler metrics, can maximize tensor-core occupancy and memory-bandwidth utilization, leading to significant latency and throughput improvements in long-context LLM inference.
Related Work: BitDecoding optimizes KV cache decoding by leveraging Tensor Cores and CUDA Cores but does not dynamically adapt to runtime hardware metrics. Spectral Latent Steering and Geometric Signal Separation propose theoretical frameworks for latency optimization but lack hardware-faithful evaluation. This proposal bridges the gap by integrating kernel-level throughput engineering with hardware-aware memory layout design.
Abstract: The rise of long-context Large Language Models (LLMs) amplifies memory and bandwidth demands during autoregressive decoding. Existing KV-cache optimization methods often neglect the interplay between kernel-level throughput and memory-bandwidth utilization. We propose a dynamic KV-cache layout optimization framework that adjusts memory access patterns based on real-time profiler metrics (e.g., warp stall reasons, arithmetic intensity) to maximize tensor-core occupancy and memory-bandwidth utilization. Our approach integrates kernel-level throughput engineering with hardware-aware memory layout design, validated through end-to-end latency and throughput benchmarks on commodity GPUs. Evaluated on LLaMA-3.1-8B with a 128K context, our framework reduces single-batch decoding latency by 3× and improves throughput by 7.5× compared to FP16 FlashDecoding-v2. We provide detailed profiler traces, including occupancy, achieved bandwidth, and warp stall reasons, to attribute gains to specific hardware causes.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Measure baseline latency and throughput of FP16 FlashDecoding-v2 on LLaMA-3.1-8B with a 128K context using nvprof/Nsight traces.
Experiment 2: Implement dynamic KV-cache layout optimization and measure latency, throughput, and hardware metrics (occupancy, achieved bandwidth, warp stall reasons) on the same setup.
Experiment 3: Conduct ablation studies to isolate the impact of individual optimizations (e.g., coalesced loads, reduced HBM round-trips) on latency and throughput.
Experiment 4: Evaluate the framework on multiple GPU architectures (e.g., Blackwell, Hopper, Ada, Ampere) to assess generalizability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk: The dynamic layout optimization may introduce overhead that offsets latency gains. Mitigation: Profile and minimize overhead during implementation.
Limitation: The framework relies on profiler metrics, which may vary across GPU architectures. Mitigation: Conduct extensive evaluation on multiple architectures.
Risk: The optimization may not generalize to all LLM architectures. Mitigation: Evaluate on diverse LLM architectures beyond LLaMA-3.1-8B.
